data.title: IYR: Sell Your REIT ETFs
data.description:
There are several large REIT ETFs now that I track, including the iShares U.S. Real Estate ETF (IYR), formed in 2000; the Real Estate Select Sector SPDR® Fund ETF (XLRE), formed in 2015; and the Vanguard Real Estate Index Fund ETF Shares (VNQ), formed in 2004. These three ETFs range in AUM size from $3.8 billion for IYR, the oldest, to $64 billion for VNQ and $7.4 billion for XLRE.

I discussed each of these a month ago, when I initiated coverage of VNQ with a Sell, and I am following up with an initiation of IYR, though all three have declined in price. While I concluded in that piece that I was negative on VNQ, I did say that the peers concerned me. In this follow up, I explain why I think that holders of IYR should reduce exposure.

A Look At IYR

BlackRock does an excellent job, in my view, of creating ETFs for investors to purchase. Their unit, iShares, launched IYR more than two decades ago, but it is the smallest of the three REIT ETFs that I follow. It is different from VNQ, which is much larger, in that its benchmark is the Dow Jones U.S. Real Estate Capped Index. You can learn more about it on the iShares website page dedicated to the ETF. There is also a Fact Sheet that was last updated on September 30.

The fee is a bit higher than the fee for VNQ or XLRE, but the 0.38% management fee is still pretty low. The ETF has 61 stocks and trades at a price/book of 2.4X, according to iShares. Here are the top 10 holdings:

These largest holdings total 50.2%, with two names above 5%, Welltower (WELL) and Prologis (PLD). These two were (and are) the largest holdings in VNQ as well. WELL, has increased 13.3% since my VNQ article, while VNQ has dropped 3.3% and IYR has declined by 3.4%. Here is the year-to-date action for the total returns of IYR, VNQ, and XLRE:

The return of the S&P 500 has been 12.3%, which is much higher than that of the REIT ETFs. REITs were historically part of the Financial sector, and the Financial Select Sector SPDR Fund (XLF), which I don't like at this price at all, has returned 6.8% year-to-date.

While Seeking Alpha analysts have a Buy rating on IYR, Seeking Alpha gives it a Strong Sell rating on quantitative measures.

Risks To IYR And The Other REITs

REITs appeal to investors because they provide income (like fixed-income funds) and capital gains (like equity funds). Bonds have been challenged for a while now due to concerns about inflation. While the Federal Reserve has been cutting rates (short-term rates), reducing Fed Funds substantially over the past year, Treasury yields have gone up since it started. I initiated coverage of two big fixed-income ETFs, the iShares Core U.S. Aggregate Bond ETF (AGG) and the Vanguard Total Bond Market ETF (BND), a few weeks ago, warning that they face risk. In that piece, I suggested that rates could rise, and they have increased across the yield curve since then. This table has Treasury yields as of last night compared to where they were ahead of the first rate-cut in September 2024:

Fed Funds has come down by 1.25% since the first cut to a range now of 3.75-4.00%. The Treasury yields seem to be pricing in additional cuts. Another challenge for fixed-income investors is that credit spreads are low. So, a big risk to IYR, other REIT ETFs, and REITs is that bonds could start to attract investors if they decline in price (rise in yield). Inflation is a big risk to fixed-income.

Recession is a big risk to stocks, as a weaker economy can mean lower earnings. The stocks that are up so much tend to be the Magnificent 7, which are dominated by Technology and Consumer Discretionary companies. I explained recently why I am negative on Technology, and the sector has started to roll over. The reaction to the good report from NVIDIA yesterday is a sign of potential challenges for technology stocks. If stocks crumble, it could lead to selling pressure on REITs.

The REIT ETFs have been in high demand from investors for many years, but they are starting to roll over. How low can they go? It's hard to tell, but they sure have gone up a lot over the past 20+ years:

373% return since the iShares Core US Aggregate Bond ETF (AGG) launched in 2003 is a big return, most of which has happened since the pandemic hit. The return of IYR since its inception in 2000 has been 637%, and it has outpaced the S&P 500 since then.

Conclusion

Some investors may like these REIT ETFs because they have lagged the overall market and Financials recently, but I caution against that. I am cautious on equities and fixed-income, and the crowd may get caught if this negative environment plays out. IYR is acting poorly, and I suggest not buying the dip.

data.title: 2 Signs Indicating That The Market Correction May Not Be Over Yet
data.description:
What we experienced yesterday was insane, to say the least. Nvidia (NVDA) opened the trading session with great optimism and a price per share close to $200, but ended the session at $181: it went from +5% to -3%. At the time I am writing the article, the pre-market signals a further 1% contraction.

The most surprising aspect is that Nvidia’s quarterly results were great, but apparently not enough to sustain the AI trend. This is typical when a bubble deflates, as the companies can no longer meet the sky-high expectations.

Anyway, I can’t predict a stock market crash, so this might just end up being a healthy correction (as it is often the case). I am not selling anything, and I am eventually willing to buy the dip. I want to make this clear. However, as I mentioned in my previous articles, I think this is the time to increase our prudence, especially when it comes to stock picking. If your friends tell you to buy a new AI stock that will surge in a couple of months, my suggestion is just to think twice.

I summarized in two aspects the reasons why I am currently adopting a risk-off approach.

Bitcoin signals a risk-off environment

I don’t remember if I ever discussed about Bitcoin (BTC:USD) on Seeking Alpha. Anyway, this is briefly what I think:

  • Its intrinsic value is $0, but people’s perception led it to what it stands for now.
  • I have never invested in Bitcoin, or any other cryptocurrencies.
  • Somehow, people believe in Bitcoin, and this is something to take into account. You can definitely make money out of it, but you are going to bear significant risk.

So, in a couple of words, Bitcoin is the ultimate “risk-on” asset. A bullish stock market is associated with Bitcoin raising prices, as well as the opposite. The correlation with the Nasdaq 100-Index (NDX) is pretty evident as they typically overlap.

In less than 2 months, Bitcoin crashed 34%, and this is definitely not a positive sign for the stock market. To avoid misunderstanding, I am not implying that Bitcoin can lead the stock market prices, but it can be treated as a sentiment indicator.

Being the ultimate risk-on asset, a strong decline means that investors are rotating their investment portfolio toward safer options; I don’t think the Nasdaq 100 belongs to that list.

Seven companies make up 65% of the entire index performance, and they are all highly involved in the AI field. We know how high the expectations are toward this industry, and what we have seen yesterday with Nvidia seems like a sign of exhaustion of the upward trend.

We’ll see, but Bitcoin drop is telling us that investors are starting to consider the flip side of the coin. The risk-on sentiment, typical of the tech industry as well, has never been questioned over the last years. Every billion invested in AI was worth it, regardless of ROI. Now the seed of doubt is gradually growing, and after such a strong rally, it might be time to slow down the enthusiasm a little bit.

However, I am not talking about a broader market crash, but a rotation of capitals.

As you can see, from early November the XLK (Tech stocks ETF) widely underperformed the XLP (Consumer staples ETF), so not all the stocks are going down.

Finally, I think we shouldn’t completely overlook the effect of such a crashing Bitcoin. According to River, almost 50 million Americans own Bitcoin, and 32 American public companies hold Bitcoin in their balance sheets.

Behind the Bitcoin crash there are people that invested their savings, companies that hold it as a Treasury asset, and a whole industry that depends on its performance. While it is very complex to predict the economic impact of its crash, we know for certain that it is not positive.

The risk-on sentiment is fading away, and the volatility is getting wild because of the huge margin debt.

The stock market has been sustained by record leverage, so even a minimal change in the sentiment might lead to a significant drawdown driven by margin calls. The leverage in the financial system needs to deflate.

Too many doubts about the U.S. economy

The stock market doesn’t like uncertainty, and we are heading toward a blurred future.

I just happened to read a recent news about President Trump's decision to remove the 40% tariff on Brazilian food imports (beef, coffee, fruits, and cocoa) to reduce rising food prices in the U.S.; this news left me perplexed.

Even though empirical evidence states otherwise, we have been told that these tariffs wouldn’t have led to higher inflation, as they are paid by foreign countries. Now we are having the first signs that this is not the case, and I think this is even more important than the Bitcoin issue regarding risk on/risk off.

If inflation experiences the second mountain, we can certainly eliminate all the chances of lowering interest rates. On the contrary, we might even experience rising interest rates, and this would definitely not benefit the economy and the stock market. Government couldn’t solve the refinancing issue, and companies would pay higher interest payments, negatively affecting the bottom line. In other words, this is the last thing we need.

On November 26th will be released the Core PCI data, previously delayed because of the government shutdown. This is the Fed’s favorite inflation gauge, and this time it seems very hard to make the correct decision. The government shutdown has caused a paucity of economic data, but the Fed has to make a choice anyway.

Inflation is giving us signs of revival, but on the other hand, we have a labor market that is facing some cracks. U.S. non-farm payrolls were better than expected (+119k actual vs. +50k consensus), but the unemployment rate delivered a small increase of 0.10%, and it is now at 4.40%, the highest level since 2021.

To be clear, 4.40% is still a pretty solid number, but take a look at the total non-farm job openings.

The number of vacant jobs is decreasing, and this historically coincided with a decline in the stock market. The AI enthusiasm supported the latter, but now the divergence is huge, and I don’t see why it shouldn’t be filled. A growing economy should experience a higher number of vacant jobs, not people that struggle to find a new job. So, either the stock market deflates its valuation or the labor market starts adding new vacant jobs.

Overall, the inflation data suggests that reducing interest rates might be a mistake, but labor market data suggests that a rate cut would help. Basically, the Fed is between the hammer and the anvil, and the risk of making the wrong monetary policy decision is pretty high.

Conclusion

The uncertainties about the U.S. economy and the switching sentiment toward risk-off assets are the reasons why I am having a prudent approach. The massive usage of leverage adds fuel to the market correction.

I didn’t sell any shares, but I am not buying either. For now, I am waiting for data that can restore my optimism: my opinion won’t change if the market bounces back over the next weeks. I am aware that I could miss some opportunities, but I don’t think this is the time to pull the trigger, as my portfolio is already skewed toward U.S. hyperscalers.

In terms of the long-term view, I don't think that much has changed. U.S. companies will still be my top holdings; I just need a bigger margin of safety to start buying U.S. hyperscalers once again.

data.title: Domo Downgraded To Sell: High Costs, Slow Revenue Growth, And Turnaround Uncertainty
data.description:
Investment Outlook

Domo, Inc. (DOMO) provides a range of data integration, analysis and visualization software to enterprises worldwide.

I previously analyzed Domo in February 2025, upgrading the stock to a Hold outlook due to improved positioning at the beginning of the enterprise AI era.

However, revenue growth continues to disappoint while operating expenses remain high.

Investor capital would be better allocated elsewhere while the company navigates significant and time-consuming changes to its business and go-to-market models, so I’ve downgraded DOMO to a Sell.

Domo’s Market And Approach

The company provides a wide range of data integration, visualization and analysis tools to help businesses improve their performance.

The company's software offerings include:

  • Data integration - hundreds of pre-built data sources.

  • Collaboration - data sharing within the organization.

  • Data visualization - visualization tools.

  • Agentic AI - ability to create automations based on company functions.

  • AI-powered insights - helping identify business patterns and trends.

According to a 2025 Mordor Intelligence market research report, the global business intelligence software and services market was an estimated $38 billion in early 2025 and is expected to reach $56 billion in 2030.

If accomplished, this growth would represent a moderately robust CAGR of 8.15% from 2025 to 2030.

The main drivers for this expected growth is robust demand from organizations for real-time information and analysis capabilities about their business operating environments to enhance their planning and decision-making processes.

The major industry sectors of Banking, Financial Services and Insurance are expected to generate strong demand through 2030, along with increasing demand from the Retail and Healthcare industries.

The North America region is expected to continue to produce the highest demand growth rate for the industry, followed by Europe, Asia-Pacific and the MENA region, as the graphic shows below:

Domo’s revenue contribution breakdown by segment and location is shown in the pie charts here:

The company’s competitor base includes the following firms or offerings:

  • Power BI (Microsoft).

  • Tableau (Salesforce).

  • Qlik.

  • SAP (SAP).

  • Looker (Google).

  • Oracle (ORCL).

  • Sisense.

  • ThoughtSpot.

  • Alteryx.

The industry for business intelligence and analytics is divided between major platform operators who can bundle in the BI/analytics functions into a larger relationship, independent full-stack players like Domo and smaller, point solution providers or data transformation specialists.

Current trends for the BI/analytics industry feature significant integration of generative AI with existing capabilities to create "copilots" and improved front-end UI, but even major players are concerned about the possibility of "disruption from below" from generative AI systems.

I’ve prepared a thumbnail SWOT of the company's primary market characteristics:

Strengths

  • Cloud-native system with strong real-time data visualization and integrations, AI-centric strategy focus, multi-cloud modern architecture.

Weaknesses

  • Large incumbent vendors who can bundle BI/analytics features, smaller orgs may have difficulty with complex system, shift to usage-based pricing may have risks.

Opportunities

  • Increasing demand for data-driven decisions, embedded analytics increases stickiness, international or industry expansion.

Threats

  • Continued changes and capabilities from general-purpose AI, intense competition in a changing industry landscape, slow transition of existing customers to consumption model.

Recent Financial Trends

Total revenue by quarter (columns) rose slightly due to new average contract value growth and higher sales productivity. Operating income by quarter (red line) has improved year-over-year because of lower SG&A comps.

Gross profit margin by quarter (blue line) has remained stable while management says it should expand longer term. Selling and G&A expenses as a percentage of total revenue by quarter (orange line) have dropped due to cost discipline and higher-than-expected revenue. R&D expenses as a function of revenue (purple line) varied within a range and are focused on AI, agentic capabilities and CDW integrations.

Earnings per share (Diluted) have remained heavily negative, with no meaningful move toward breakeven.

(All data in the above charts is GAAP.)

For balance sheet results, Domo ended the quarter with $47.1 million in cash and equivalents and $125.6 million in total debt, of which $3.7 million was due within twelve months.

In the past 12 months, DOMO’s stock price has risen by 25% vs. that of the benchmark iShares Expanded Technology-Software ETF’s (IGV) fall of 2%, as the chart indicates below.

Below is a helpful financial and operating metrics table for Domo:

(Source - Seeking Alpha.)

The company's GAAP Rule of 40 performance in the past five quarters is shown in the following graphic, with no revenue growth combined with a negative operating margin producing negative, albeit improving, results:

Capital Allocation

Over the trailing twelve months, free cash flow was only $12.5 million, and capital expenditures were $10.0 million.

However, the company paid a hefty $57.4 million in stock-based compensation ("SBC") in the last four quarters, so free cash flow net of SBC was negative ($44.9 million).

The net dollar decrease in stock outstanding for the last four quarters was valued at $0.7 million.

SBC as a percentage of revenue over the trailing twelve-month period was 18%, a high figure for a company of Domo’s stage of maturity.

Management’s plans for capital allocation in relation to shares remain quiet, although it has set up an at-the-market Controlled Equity Offering Sales Agreement program, which can authorize the potential sale of up to $150 million of Class B common stock as part of the up to $300 million of securities the company registered on Sept. 20, 2024 (Source - SEC S-3/A)

Overall, Domo is paying a significant amount to its employees via stock, but it isn’t really a "growth" stock from its lack of revenue growth trajectory.

As a result, its capital allocation approach is heavy on SBC expenses but currently neutral on the share count.

Why I’m Bearish On Domo

Domo has grown its remaining performance obligations [RPO] to over $409 million as it focuses on multi-year, strategic contracts and improving expansion through a consumption-centric model.

It is seeing customer who began on its consumption model producing a 108% net dollar retention rate, much higher than its ARR NDRR of only 94%.

Internationally, especially in Japan, Domo’s offerings are performing well amid very high renewal net retention rates.

Domo’s current growth strategy features a desire to shift its existing clients toward a consumption model, growing its integration and partner ecosystem, targeting enterprise customers for data strategy and governance and expanding into embedded analytics and low-code app development.

However, the company needs to execute well on its CDW/hyperscaler partnerships, which require hiring more technical personnel for integrations and co-selling.

And transitioning existing customers to its consumption model may limit uplift due to caps or contract structures.

Domo has struggled to make progress toward profitability for a wide range of reasons, including an elevated cost structure in high SG&A and R&D, high customer churn as evidenced in its low net dollar retention rate and a slow transition to its consumption approach.

Regarding valuation, the market is valuing DOMO at a forward EV/Sales multiple of only 1.8x on an estimated NTM revenue growth rate of an anemic 0.8% against a median Meritech Capital SaaS Index implied ARR growth rate of approximately 15% (Source).

Also, the benchmark index indicates a recent median Net Dollar Retention Rate of 110% vs. Domo’s ARR NDRR of just 94%. A result under 100% means net churn and is a disappointing result.

The index of publicly held SaaS application software companies showed a forward EV/Revenue multiple median of around 4.9x on October 31, 2025, as the chart shows here:

So, by comparison, DOMO is currently being valued by public investors at a large discount to the broader index as of October 31, 2025 due to its poor revenue growth rate and low ARR net dollar retention rate.

Looking ahead, Domo has guided to growing billings and revenue, with $319 million and $318 million, respectively.

Still, that revenue growth expectation is only 0.8% versus the sector median growth rate of 7.8%, suggesting continued underperformance, even if the company hits management’s guidance.

Domo appears to be shifting toward a channel-led go-to-market model while transitioning its customer base to a consumption revenue model, all in an effort to turn the company around.

The jury is still well out on that turnaround and the stock appears to be sagging after a mid-2025 runup on investor impatience or flagging interest.

I believe Domo will continue to underwhelm in the near term as I see continued high costs and a slow to nearly non-existent revenue growth trajectory.

As such, investors would likely be better off deploying their capital elsewhere, so my outlook is downgraded to a Sell.

data.title: Pagaya: Its Operating Results Are Stellar: Its Stock Price Less So!
data.description:
Pagaya-Great quarter and strong outlook but a modest share price response-the recipe for a great investment

This few weeks have seen a substantial pullback in equities. From record closes for the Dow Index earlier at the start of November to substantial losses, particularly last Thursday. In writing about almost any growth stock acknowledgement needs to be made that they won’t go up in a toxic market environment. And the past two weeks have seen a toxic market environment for growth stocks.

This is an article about Pagaya (PGY) and not AI or other bubbles. But the core of everything that Pagaya does is indeed AI and it sometimes gets valued as an AI stock.

I am not going to attempt to analyze the panic that has set in amongst investors for much of the past week regarding bubbles, valuation and the AI trade. While I read articles expressing the skepticism some investors express about the “AI Trade” that we all do, I also look at empirical evidence about actual end-user demand for end-user solutions utilizing AI technology which is and remains-exceptionally strong. Sentiment about bubble valuations and bubble conditions seems to me more than a bit misplaced. Many revolutions dating back decades in technology have had their doubters. I believe that AI is such a revolution and is, in no sense the chimera that some have opined. But this is not an article attempting to answer questions about bubbles and market valuation, but an article recommending the purchase of Pagaya shares.

Pagaya is not literally an AI stock or company. It is a sub-prime lender using AI technology to more accurately predict the results of borrower applications brought to the company by its partners. It has been very successful in achieving its strategic goals and it has just reported and provided a strong outlook. While that might sound the same as the situation with Palantir (PLTR), the difference is relative valuation with Palantir having the highest valuation and Pagaya the lowest.

While I acknowledge Palantir to be an incredibly successful company with unique technology and an exceptional competitive position, Pagaya in its own way has some similar components to its investment story. I just want to be clear that I am not trying to compare Palantir to Pagaya. Other than that their names both start with “P” their businesses have nothing whatsoever to do with each other…except that they are both built around the effective use of AI technology.

Pagaya recently reported its results. The quarter was one that exceeded guided metrics and GAAP earnings were guided higher for Q4. And yet despite positive results and a strong forecast the shares have fallen. It can be frustrating or something worse to try to figure out why. Pagaya reported its results on November 10th. In an abundance of prudence, not all metrics were guided higher. Still, an EV/S of approximately 1.4X (that's based on my assumption of 4Q forward revenue of $1.5 billion, and diverges modestly from the published 1st Call consensus, with a P/E based on the annualized current non-GAAP earnings of $4.08 that is just greater than 5X. Since this is the 1st year in which the company has had meaningful non-GAAP earnings, historical P/E analysis is not meaningful.

The company forecast revenue growth for the quarter of about 27%, based on the CFOs expectation for sustained growth in both auto and POS lending, and no increase in personal loans.

The shares were up by a bit less than 13% on the Monday following the earnings release. Of course that is a pretty decent one day increase, but the reality is that the results reported were so strong that a more significant appreciation was probably warranted. But selling has resumed and the shares have been falling persistently and have reached an apogee of selling on 11/13 in the wake of a SEC 13 G/A filing-which while containing no new news has been quite misinterpreted and seems to have been the focus of negative theories. For subscribers who can tolerate some risk and some possible pain, the current selling excess would be an excellent entry point. As I will be at pains to show, the erstwhile negative theories regarding Pagaya, and there have been many of these, have seemingly been laid to rest.

While Pagaya shares were up nicely in the wake of their earnings report that gain has now been completely lost and reversed. I would note two additional points: the shares are down by more than 53% from their high point less than 2 months ago and more important is that Pagaya’s valuation metrics have compressed and are far less than almost any other comparable company-indeed almost any company.

Pagaya, despite its valuation, is considered a “risk-on” investment. Over the past 10 days or so, investors have been selling risk-on investments more or less indiscriminately. By indiscriminately, I mean with a seeming disregard for corporate news. This is not the place to debate sentiment-which has self-evidently pivoted away from anything tech or so-called bubbles. It would, I imagine, be particularly difficult to describe Pagaya’s valuation as bubble like, or even microscopically inflated. But the shares are simply not going to perform based on logical financial analysis during risk-off periods in the market.

The most recent fear for stocks of all kinds is based on angst that the Fed will not cut rates at its December meeting. This is particularly true for many lenders and particularly sub-prime lenders such as Pagaya. The concept is that essentially, if the economy is not bad enough, and job losses are not worrisome enough, then a majority of Fed governors will not vote to cut rates because of inflationary concerns. I won’t try to discuss that concept here other than to say it wouldn’t be my favorite scenario in terms of Fed decision making. What I will say is that if the economy is strong enough, then the concept that Pagaya, as a sub-prime lender, is vulnerable to macro headwinds simply can’t hold water.

As of this writing on 11/21/25, the FedWatch tool, basically a reflection of the future’s market for interest rates, has a 76% probability of a rate cut at the December FOMC meeting, up materially since the start of the week That percentage expectation for a rate cut rises to 83% by the time of the January meeting and to 91% at the time of the March meeting. Expectations for rate cuts have been volatile, and despite the government reopening, much data has yet to be compiled and released.

I think it is fair to say that Pagaya shares have had a remarkably compressed valuation for all the time I have followed them. Some of the time a compressed valuation might have made sense. Part of the time investors and analysts were concerned that the company’s performance would be constrained by required statutory risk retention requirements. As that concern ebbed with the advent of a far more diversified funding structure for the company that now includes a variety of sources such as direct investment, forward flow agreements and balance sheet sources, the company went through a period in which it dealt with elevated impairment charges that essentially wiped out returns from some loans made during the 2021-23 time span. And that too, is now behind the company.

Pagaya has an elevated short interest. 15% of the outstanding shares and 25% of the float were reported as short at the end of October. Usually short sellers respond to a string of good quarters by looking elsewhere to place their negative bets. That seemingly isn’t happening so far. The short thesis for Pagaya, and other sub-prime lenders relates to an set of beliefs that in a deteriorating economic environment, Pagaya’s borrower cohorts will be more vulnerable than other borrowers. And short sellers have been emboldened by the failure of sub-prime auto lenders such as Tricolor Holdings and Automotive Credit Corp. The specifics of Pagaya’s auto lending business are vastly different, but so far the message hasn’t gotten through to short sellers. It seems likely that short sellers have been pressing their bets.

Also part of the pattern that some perceive have been poor results of some regional lenders. While that proved to be a few days sensation, ultimately most regional lenders did just fine, although this angst spilled over and has been a factor in Pagaya's share price decline. Just looking at the reality vs. fears suggest some kind of massive sentiment overhang unrelated to any sort of reality.

The recent data from non-government sources that suggests that unemployment is rising. Short sellers apparently are looking for bets that they believe are well correlated with rising unemployment and Pagaya is one such bet. I believe that is why short sellers have been reluctant to cover their Pagaya positions and one reason why shares have been so pressured despite the strength of the company’s recent earning report. Logically, it is difficult to believe that the economy is too strong to precipitate rate cuts but also so weak as to foster massive credit defaults by some prime borrowers. But logic is not always a precipitating factor in valuing stocks day to day.

Pagaya shares have also been tarred by a brush that has unveiled when Upstart (UPST) had a quarter that missed expectations. Never mind that the Upstart quarter was the result of a model that was improperly trained and provided what the co-founder and CTO of that company called a false positive in terms of a pending recession-if Upstart missed its quarter, then surely, so the argument runs, Pagaya must be vulnerable.

Even though Pagaya shares are up by more than 100% year to date, these last three months, as mentioned have seen drawdowns based on either facts that aren’t valid, misplaced correlations and a search for negative bets that defies logic and which are based on sentiment and momentum. And that, I believe, is the investment opportunity at this time. I am rating Pagaya shares as a strong buy and have amassed a substantial weight in Pagaya shares in my portfolio. I am only too aware that sentiment can always trump reality in the short term. If that weren’t the case, it would be hard to deliver differentiated returns.

Sometimes sentiment can overweigh reality for quarters at a time. I am prepared to be patient. In fact I already have had to be patient. But that said, I am recommending purchase of Pagaya at the current time-11/21/25 and at the current price-a bit less than $21/share.

I last wrote about Pagaya on the pages of Seeking Alpha back in February. The shares have done well since that point, but the irony, at least to this author, is that the shares, looking at a variety of valuation metrics are cheaper now than was the case back in February. I do have a significant position in Pagaya shares-I did so in February and I have more so now. So, I want to be careful to avoid confirmation bias. But in terms of almost all valuation metrics that I consider, Pagaya’s valuation has compressed substantially. Even the P/E ratio, something rarely considered by most investors these days, has reached a value level. Last quarter, the company reported non-GAAP EPS of $1.02. So, on an annualized basis, the P.E is just greater than 5X. The P/EG ratio is well below 1X.

Pagaya’s relationship with the investment community has probably been rockier than it has needed to be. Much of that is a self-inflicted wound. By that I refer to management’s lack of transparency and guidance during the period when the company was reporting elevated impairment charges. But with the last several earnings releases, the company and its management seem to have found some religion with regards to both prudence and transparency. There is very little beyond the disclosure of Pagaya’s newest partners that was left undocumented or unsaid during the course of the most recent earnings release. These partners have to give their permission for press releases and sometimes that takes time, but the company specifically reported 8 in-process on-boardings, a record for the company with a strong pipeline of additional new partners in negotiation.

In the course of writing this article, Viola Ventures filed amendment 4 relating to its holdings of Pagaya. The summary of the filing is as follows:

A Schedule 13G/A filed with the SEC on November 13, 2025, shows that while certain Viola entities no longer hold Class A ordinary shares directly, other entities, namely Viola Partners 5, L.P. and Viola Partners Fund 4 2013 L.P., still own shares.

Here are the key details from the November 13, 2025, SEC filing:

Viola Ventures IV entities completed transactions in August and September 2025 that resulted in a complete disposition of their direct holdings of Class A ordinary shares. These weren’t sales but were transfers to the underlying partners as required by Viola’s structure as it has neared the time for its winding up.

Following these transactions, some shares were transferred to other Viola entities.

Viola Partners 5, L.P. holds 76,194 Class A ordinary shares, and Viola Partners Fund 4 2013 L.P. holds 129,932 Class A ordinary shares.

Individual partners, such as Avi Zeevi, also have beneficial ownership of additional shares that have been transferred to them.

My guess is that this filing and the anticipation of the filing have been significant factors in the decline of Pagaya shares in the wake of the earnings release. Of course that is a guess-and often selling begets selling. One of Viola’s partnerships, Viola IV, distributed shares a couple of months ago and that apparently started the downward pressure on Pagaya shares. So, in essence, this is an update of that filing. It doesn’t appear to have been any additional shares distributed.

Avi Zeevi is a Pagaya board member and has been a founder of the Viola Group. He has been a Pagaya director since 2016. His only listed transaction in Pagaya shares was a purchase that took place on April 4, 2004 when he bought 30k shares.

On the day the filing was made, Pagaya shares were active and declined noticeably. I believe that this was partially a factor of misreading the specific of the

It has been painful to watch Pagaya shares melt, presumably in response to this filing and fears about additional Pagaya shares in the market and now fears about US interest rates. But since the filing, the market itself has been in a bit of a panic mode with everything associated with risk being for sale. I know this sounds anodyne-but as a long-term investor I have to believe that irrational selling is an opportunity. Once, that is, after I get over that sinking feeling in the pit of my own quite voluminous stomach.

What does Pagaya do and how is that different than what investment peers do?

At its core, Pagaya is defined as a sup-prime lender with offerings in personal loans, auto loans and what it calls POS, which is basically the equivalent to Buy Now/Pay Later transactions. Pagaya calls its personal loan and auto loan process decline monetization-basically what it is doing is taking the vast cohort of loan declines from its partners and processing these through its AI engines to see if there are potential loan customers that it can approve after they have been declined by the tools available to its loan origination partners. Not terribly surprisingly, the approval rate is low although the opportunity set is very large.

This is a substantially different model than that deployed by Upstart (UPST) and other competitors in the space. Pagaya’s loan origination partners send it millions of applications each quarter. This, in itself, provides the company with a huge amount of data that is an unheralded advantage that the company enjoys when building its credit models. Pagaya does little or no marketing-certainly no marketing to consumers-and it is not and never will be a consumer brand although it has branded some of its credit offerings.

A key metric for this company is the number of its partners, each of whom is large. The company has kept adding partners at a significant pace and now is in the process of on-boarding 8 new partners with a significant queue set to start the process over the next year. That is actually a record for the company in terms of the number of partners. Many of these partners ultimately contribute $1 billion or more of network volume-currently 5 loan origination partners have crossed this $1 billion threshold.

Because it is a “second look” company, its acceptance rate is low-it has been around 1% for some years now now. Pagaya has additional products beyond that of second look. It has what is called an Affiliate Optimizer Engine. This is technology that takes the “declined” customer to other channels such as Credit Karma, LendingTree (TREE) and Experian (OTCQX:EXPGY) amongst others automatically to determine if the potential borrower can secure a loan through those channels.

Pagaya has what it calls a Direct Marketing Engine. This is an app based on AI and the company’s data estate that allows its partners to prescreen potential borrowers and to target creditworthy potential borrowers and provide customized offers for these clients.

Our Prescreen product has been under development for nearly 2 years. Optimized for campaign management through direct mail & email, Prescreen enables partners to proactively engage with prospective and existing customers with extended credit through a personal loan. We estimate the incremental impact for an average mid-sized lender could equate to several hundred thousand customers per year

I was surprised to learn that more than half of Pagaya’s network volume in personal loans is coming from offerings other than decline monetization. At this point, while only 30% of Pagaya’s partners use multiple products they account for more than 2/3rds of the company’s network volume. In considering what Pagaya’s CAGR will be, a major, and probably under-appreciated tailwind is this multi-product opportunity, a key initiative for this company.

At this point, 32% of Pagaya’s network volume is coming from its products addressing POS and autos, up from 9% in the year earlier period. With the recent forward flow deal, and the residual certificate deal with One William Street, the company should actually see an acceleration in auto lending, which grew 10% sequentially last quarter. POS volume grew 17%, albeit from a relatively small base and the company has sold a $300 million AAA rated ABS backed by POS loans earlier this month.

How fast can Pagaya grow?

Pagaya basically has 3 legs to its growth stool. It is likely to continue to add new loan origination partners to the 31 it has acknowledged. As mentioned, the company is onboarding a record new 8 partners and it closed two additional partners last quarter. It has indicated that it will meet its target to close 2-4 new partners both this year and next with currently initiated negotiations. It isn’t these new partners that are driving growth; it is basically the partners that have been on-boarded in the past 12-18 months and where volumes are starting to meaningfully ramp. Pagaya has not been announcing the names of its newest partners-presumably because it wants to have them on-board and contributing network volume.

Selling existing customers additional products is both capturing the lowest hanging fruit but also the most significant revenue growth opportunity in the short term. The additional services described above are seamless for users and the prebuilt APIs allow for clients to start using these products almost immediately.

Finally, the growth of the company’s auto and POS lending products is significantly greater than the corporate average. According to the 1st call published revenue growth consensus, Pagaya’s growth will be between 15%-17% next year. The company hasn’t provided a 2026 forecast for revenues. Because of recent trends in the company's POS and auto lending growth, I have used a CAGR of 27%, congruent with the recent past and with the company's detailed Q4 forecast.

The company’s forecast for Q4 is for consistent growth in Auto and POS lending and for no sequential growth in personal loans. That projection would take revenue to $370 million, or growth of 27% year on year and 6% sequentially. I am using a 27% 3 year CAGR; I think that is probably conservative given the new customers in queue and those in the onboarding process coupled with the new SKUs such as Prescreen that are achieving notable traction but the company itself is choosing to be prudent in terms of its revenue forecast in the wake of the murky economic outlook

Macro trends and overall consumer behavior remain healthy, and we continue to monitor closely through the data advantage we have of working with 31 different partners across multiple asset classes. We're always ready to shift if and when needed.

Our full year 2025 outlook reflects the momentum and resilience in our business to-date and our unique operating leverage, while maintaining our cautious stance given the protracted volatility.

In the wake of some high profile issues in the subprime lending space over the last 90 days, a cautious projection makes sense. That doesn’t really mean that it is terribly likely-especially if macro trends do not deteriorate.

More speculative, is the news about a potential $2000 tariff rebate check also being floated. When a similar stimulus was paid in 2021, it had very positive effects on the sub-prime lending business and dropped impairment charges dramatically. I have no idea beyond the news that I read with regards to the likelihood of a $2000 tariff rebate payment. No one should go out and buy Pagaya shares just because of a possible rebate payment. Similarly, I think the concern that the economy will fall into a significant recession and in turn cause Pagaya to absorb excess impairment charges is a chimera in the minds of aggressive short sellers.

While it is typical to focus on revenue growth when looking at most companies, the focus of Pagaya is to maximize the growth of its fee revenue less production cost (the abbreviation for this is FRLPC). Basically, that is the equivalent of a gross margin metric. It is the take rate from originating loans for its loan origination partners. Looked at holistically, an analysis of Pagaya’s revenue growth starts with the network volume generated by its platform. Network volume grew by 19% last quarter and is projected to grow 19% for the full year. From network volume, Pagaya generates fee revenue from network volume. Last quarter fee revenues were $340 million or growth of 36% year on year. Fee revenue growth has been far stronger than network growth because of growing revenues from customers using multi products and increasing volumes from existing customers. FRLPC, in turn rose by 39% year over year, with growth accelerating notably this past quarter. Overall, the sequential growth in FRLPC was 10% in the September quarter and the growth of 39% compares to growth of 29% in the prior quarter.

Last quarter, the FRLPC margin was 5% of network volume a record high for Pagaya. This is the top of Pagaya’s forecast range. As POS and Auto grow as a percentage of total network volume, this metric will decline-at least for a time. The company’s objective is to grow FRLPC in dollar terms as fast as possible and the path to that involves an emphasis on the company’s newer verticals. I will touch on this further along in this article when considering valuation, but it is fairly unique to see a company whose gross margin growth is consistently multiple points greater than revenue. In considering relative valuation looking at the EV/S ratio this unusual trend should, I believe, be considered. Of course given that the current level of EV/S 1.4X it isn’t really necessary to do that in reaching a conclusion that the shares are mispriced and materially undervalued.

Pagaya’s Business Model-Continuing to evolve positively

About a year ago this time, Pagaya projected that it would reach and sustain GAAP profitability during 2025. At the time, during the period when the company was reporting impairment charges that emboldened short sellers and kept institutions from buying the shares, that forecast was greeted with marked skepticism and the shares reached a nadir at the start of this calendar year.

This latest quarter was the third quarter in a row in which GAAP profitability has been reported and this was one metric for which the company has increased its forecast. Overall the GAAP margin was 6% this quarter up from 5% last quarter and compared to a loss of 26% in the year earlier period. The formula has been strikingly simple. Typically, growth in network volume has been the metric that moves operating expenses. But lately the company has been managing operating expenses such that they have been noticeably falling as a percent of revenue and revenue less production costs. Operating expenses were around 49% of RLTC this quarter compared to 86% of RLTC in the year earlier period.

Overall, last quarter GAAP opex of $69.7 million was about 20% of reported revenues-that is a decline 1900 basis points year over year and at the lowest level since the company has been public. The ratio fell by 400 bps sequentially, mainly due to a significant sequential decline in G&A spending.

The company’s impairment charge was 5.8% of revenue this quarter compared to 31.2% of revenue in the year earlier period. This ratio has been fairly consistent throughout 2025. The company incurred significant one-time charges this last quarter mainly related to loan issuance costs offset in part by an income-tax settlement. The company’s operating cash flow has grown substantially so far in 2025. The company’s operating cash flow margin for the first 9 months of the year has been about 16.5% compared to 2.6% the prior year. Cash flow was particularly strong last quarter, mainly because of the strong level of GAAP net income-the company’s operating cash flow margin reached to nearly 19%.

Over the course of the quarter, the company made a number of transactions to rationalize its balance sheet and to reduce its net interest costs. Overall, the company now has about 92 million shares outstanding. The company’s net increase in its loan balance rose by a bit less than $40 million sequentially, about $26 million less than the cash flow from operations, and in line with the company’s self-funding goal.

Current trends suggest that the company will be able to self-fund substantial growth for the foreseeable future minimizing requirements for any additional equity dilution.

The company does use stock based compensation and the reported ratio of SBC/Revenue was 4.3% last quarter.

Competition and Comparisons

Although Pagaya is obviously a sub-prime lender and a second chance lender, it is a rather unique company at some level. Most lists of competitors include Upstart, Affirm (AFRM), LendingTree and Avant (OTCQB:AVAI). These are competitors in the sense that they address the same set of potential borrowers but they do so directly which means that their financial models are very different and will always be very different.

When it evaluating to competitors, the issue is that of the predictive power of the models these companies employ. Upstart has a true prodigy, Paul Gu-now 34 years old. No doubt that Mr. Gu is one of the leading lights in terms of the use of AI and machine learning to build models that make loan decisions for Upstart. Mr. Gu has gotten considerable press and investors respect his capabilities. That is not to say that the models constructed by him and his team are infallible or haven’t recently produced what Upstart described as a false positive, in turn leading to disappointing financial results.

Pagaya has its own equivalent to Paul Gu, cofounder Avital Pardo. Mr. Pardo has been with Pagaya for almost 10 years at this point, and before that he worked at a start-up called Fundbox, a small business lending platform that has been built into lending offers made by companies such as Intuit and SoFi.

Affirm is a competitor of many of Pagaya’s new POS customers. (I have a significant weight in Affirm shares in my portfolio and have owned a position of different sizes for years at this point.) Affirm is led by co-founder Max Levchin, another well-known technologist. One of Affirm’s significant differentiators is the quality of its credit models. Affirm is of course the leader in the Buy Now/Pay Later space-what Pagaya refers to as POS.

While the accuracy of models is a key competitive differentiator, I don’t think it is feasible for outsiders-and that includes this writer-to assess whose model is better in the accuracy of predictions. Over time, the likelihood is that model accuracies will converge.

So what is Pagaya’s competitive moat? It is the major partnerships it has and is developing. These include relationships with Ally Financial (ALLY), OneMain Financial, SoFi (SOFI), US. Bank (USB), Westlake Financial (WLK) amongst 31 disclosed partners and another 8 or more new, undisclosed partners. While it doesn’t get the second look for all of the borrowers of all of these companies, it has been receiving a growing proportion of their applications for second looks. In addition, Pagaya’s partners are the ones who are the targets for multi-product relationships that include PGY Prescreen and Pagaya Optimizer engine. Installing these extensions to Pagaya's core functionality will increase the stickiness of the company's relationships with its lending initiation partners.

At this point, 5 of the company’s partners are each contributing in excess of $1 billion in network volume, the goal is to steadily increase that number. In my opinion these strong partner relations are an incredible competitive moat and one that seemingly is not appropriately valued by the market at this time. These relationships are likely to remain sticky and I expect that most Pagaya loan origination partners will continue to achieve significant expansion over time.

Pagaya’s Credit Performance and its capital funding progress

The biggest risk for any sub-prime lender relates to the performance of its assets and its ability to raise capital on favorable terms so that it can achieve margin objectives while growing its book of business. Pagaya is classified as a sub-prime lender. It is different from most of those such as Upstart in that while it carries some required risk retention assets on its balance sheet, its business focus is really that of a 2nd look provider for its many FI partners. That said, credit performance is a key metric to consider in evaluating this investment. Pagaya went through a period in which its financial objectives were at extreme risk because it didn't provide a buffer in analyzing credit and thus incurred massive impairment charges. That is not currently the case.

Credit performance remains in line with expectations across Auto, Personal Loan and POS, and well within our disciplined risk tolerance.

As a reminder, while macro and credit performance are robust, we are underwriting with a cushion against potential shifts. Since the beginning of the year, we have been managing the business and positioning our portfolio accordingly and we have reflected potential future losses in our guidance as shown in our earnings supplements

One reason why Pagaya shares reached their nadir earlier this year was the elevated level of impairment charges that had been brought on by a lack of a buffer regarding defaults. Last quarter, the company incurred impairment charges of $20 million down from $77.5 million the prior year. So far this year, impairment charges have totaled $63 million. For several quarters now the company has been showing the decline in 2024 vintage losses compared to prior periods.

For personal loans, cumulative net losses are almost 40% below the peak levels of 2021. Auto loan CNLs are trending 50-65% lower than in peak periods. It is this credit performance that allowed Pagaya to raise capital through AAA rated ABS sales.

The company reports its loss rates in its supplemental financial disclosure. Loss rates last quarter fell to 17.2% down from 17.6% for the first half of the year. Prepayments rose to 14.5%. Income statement related impairment charges were $20 million last quarter compared $17 million the prior quarter and to $35 million in the first quarter of the year. The company’s CFO, has forecast that the Scenario A projection in the supplemental disclosure is on track-this is the more favorable estimate of impairment charges for the full year.

When I first starting covering Pagaya a huge risk was that of risk retention. ABS issuers are required to retain about 5% of the amount they sell on their balance sheet to meet statutory risk retention requirements. At that time, given the scale of the business which was far from profitability, it appeared as though the growth the company forecast would be constrained by the lack of capital to fund those requirements. It didn’t quite happen, but it was a near-run thing.

While any lender has to raise capital to fund its loan commitments, this is no longer a huge issue for Pagaya. Last quarter the company sold $1.8 billion of ABS to fund future loan demands. The company funds loans prior to the time it approves borrowers. The company also partnered with Castlelake which is to purchase up to $500 million of forward flow auto loans. One William Street purchased $400 million of residual certificates for Pagaya’s auto loan book.

The company has partially satisfied its capital requirements through loans from a revolving credit facility. And it also sold $500 million of senior unsecured notes to strengthen and rationalize its balance sheet. With these transaction made with Citizens, TD Bank, Wells Fargo and Texas Capital Bank, interest rates were reduced by 35%. Overall, net interest expense from these transactions should reduce interest costs by $40 million on an annual basis starting in the current quarter. The company’s balance sheet is far more securely funded and flexible than has ever been the case since I have started to follow this company.

Risks to the Investment Thesis

Pagaya is a sub-prime lender and the principle risk to the investment thesis for any of these companies is credit performance. Investors are concerned that if unemployment rises and the economy enters a recession, Pagaya will sustain an increase in impairment charges that will reduce its cash flow and GAAP income.

Pagaya’s credit analysis is based on sophisticated use of AI. While AI is based on advanced data science it can and has made mistakes that could be costly and take time to rectify. In that regard, company co-founder, Avital Pardo is a key employee whose departure would be a significant risk.

Pagaya’s growth is predicated on adding relationships with new loan origination partners and selling those partners additional products beyond credit analysis and origination for loan decline customers. This in turn requires sales execution which is always a risk.

Pagaya has been successful recently in broadening its access to capital through the negotiation of forward flow agreements with a variety of capital sources. It will need to continue to do so with favorable terms in order to continue to grow its business.

There are many sub-prime lenders and Pagaya competes in a crowded space. That said, it really competes in a particular niche in a crowded space and isn’t attempting to generate consumer demand for its services which is quite different than Upstart and many other sub-prime lenders.

Recently, some auto lenders and some smaller regional banks have either liquidated or reported disappointing results because of their impairment charges. This can be read through to Pagaya and is a risk to the shares if not the business.

Finally, Pagaya has been seen by some short sellers as a vehicle they have used to hedge bets on economic deterioration. While the correlation doesn’t seem to be well though through, the fact is that it has persisted for some times and has limited the valuation.

Wrapping Up-The case to buy Pagaya shares at this time

Pagaya reported its Q3 results on November 10th, 2025. It might have seemed like a bit of an April Fool’s joke as the shares, after initially rising on the back of a strong quarter and an upside forecast have basically turned turtle and have given back more than all of the initial appreciation. Along the way came an SEC filing that basically reprised data released months ago, but was misread as indicating that there would be more Pagaya shares available in the market.

Pagaya is the leading “2nd Look” sub-prime lender. Its business consists of helping more than 30 FIs reconsider declined loan applications using advanced AI models and deep machine learning. The company has gone through a bit of a checkered history since it went public via the SPAC route. First it had a constant drip of selling shareholders, then came concerns about raising required risk retention capital and then the most serious issue emerged in which the company faced elevated impairment charges due to not providing an adequate buffer during periods of rising rates.

The reality is that all of those issues have been overcome, albeit not without some serious investor angst. This last earnings release continues the positive trend with loan loss percentages declining and a strong increase in both FRLPC and actual revenue. This led to a 92% increase in EBITDA year on year and quarterly EPS of $1.02-which equates on an annualized basis to a P/E of just greater than 5X.

Looking forward the company is on-boarding 8 new partners and it is seeing its newer offerings such as its direct marketing engine (Prescreen) and its Affiliate Optimizer Engine which broadens the range of potential lenders who can use the Pagaya AI technology to evaluate potential borrowers who fall outside their own credit box. Further, the company’s newer verticals such as auto lending and POS lending are growing at a very rapid pace, helping to drive the company’s expected CAGR to the mid 20% range.

It is the combination of a mid-20% CAGR, rising RLTC margins and increasing free cash flow that leads to me to recommend the shares. No doubt, Pagaya share have been and are likely to remain volatile and will respond to many half-baked and totally inaccurate rumors. But in the long term, perhaps the next 12 months, the facts are what they are-and the facts show that Pagaya is executing well and overcoming the risks and uncertainties that have plagued the shares. I reiterate my buy rating on Pagaya shares and have acquired an oversize position for my portfolio.

data.title: Alphabet: Still Being Underestimated
data.description:
The Alphabet Investment Thesis

Alphabet Inc. (GOOG) has built a huge infrastructure moat, which gives them a significant advantage in the future. This advantage is based on undersea cables, fibers and cloud architecture. If we combine this with YouTube, Gemini, and the traditional search business, then we see that Alphabet is one of the few companies that are already benefiting from and generating profits thanks to AI.

The oft-mentioned statement that companies are not yet making profits with AI is therefore not true if we look at Alphabet. However, despite this, the market continues to underestimate Alphabet's position and does not give the company enough credit. In my opinion, Alphabet will beat the analysts EPS estimates and will continue to expand its multiple.

In short, Alphabet remains undervalued in the long term.

My previous coverage of Alphabet

I wrote my last article about Alphabet in May this year. At the time, I thought the shares were significantly undervalued. Firstly, because I saw AI as a positive growth driver for Alphabet, as it enabled them to place ads more effectively. Secondly, AI simplifies content creation, which is beneficial for YouTube.

At the time, I also considered multiple expansion for Alphabet to be relatively likely, and this has materialized since then. Overall, I must say that I am surprised by how quickly the sentiment towards Alphabet has changed. I would have thought it would take the market longer to become more positive about the company again.

But the reason I'm providing an update on the latest quarterly figures is that I still believe the market has not fully recognized Alphabet's potential.

Alphabet's Q3 Results

Alphabet had a $100 billion revenue quarter, growing revenue by 16% year over year, and I believe that growth is far from over. The fear that Alphabet would suffer losses in the search business due to large language model ("LLM") competition has not materialized. Much closer to the truth is that Alphabet and Meta Platforms (META) are probably the two companies that can best leverage AI at the moment.

I mean with the help of AI, Alphabet can target and improve its ads much better, so Google Search, YouTube, and the mobile applications benefit greatly. I also think that the AI overview above the search results in Google Search was a game changer. This has greatly improved the search function and made it competitive with LLMs. This way, we don't have to open a new app or website and still get answers to questions that the regular Google search wouldn't have answered satisfactorily a few months ago.

In terms of market share, Google remains the uncontested market leader with a 90% share of the search engine market. But I think it would be interesting to see how this differs when compared with various LLMs rather than just other search engines.

What many people are not aware of is that Alphabet is not only the market leader in the search engine sector, but also in the streaming sector. With 12.6%, they even have a higher market share than Netflix (NFLX), which only reached 8.3%. If we now also pay attention to Android and Chrome, we see that Alphabet has considerable moats that give it a market presence that is unparalleled.

I mean it will probably be difficult to find someone who does not come into contact with at least one Alphabet product every day. Even more so, given that Alphabet's cloud products are of great importance to many companies. Google Cloud continues to be the third largest player in the cloud market with a 13% market share. And I firmly believe that Gemini and its Enterprise AI agents will give Alphabet a huge competitive advantage in the cloud sector and will help Alphabet to gain market share in the future.

With the release of Gemini 3, Alphabet has shown that they are currently the best in the field of AI. But I believe the market has not yet fully understood this, as we will see later in the peer comparison section. To me it looks like the market continues to underestimate Alphabet and sees them as the underdog.

I think it's clear that Alphabet is profiting from AI, as evidenced by the increased Paid-Clicks, Cost-per-Click, and Cost-per-Impression figures, all of which have risen due to improved ad targeting. So as you can see, monetization at Alphabet is constantly improving thanks to AI.

Although we briefly discussed the fact that Google Cloud is only third in the cloud market, its sales in the cloud segment still increased by 34% year on year. And as I said before, I believe that growth in the cloud sector will pick up again because of Gemini Enterprise.

Google Cloud is the fastest-growing segment, and luckily, the margins of the segment are improving all the time. This means that we can assume that the probability is high that Alphabet's operating margin will improve. While the operating margin of the cloud segment was still at ~12% (4,019/31,272) over the first nine months of 2024, it is now already at ~20% (8,597/41,041) in 2025. But I see further upside in the form of an operating margin of between 30% and 40%, as this is roughly where Azure and AWS are in terms of operating margin.

The continued growth of the cloud business is also evident in the strong $157 billion revenue backlog, most of which is cloud revenue.

What else does GOOG have to offer?

In my opinion, the acquisition of Wiz will strengthen Alphabet's cloud security offering, initially generating $700 million in recurring revenue. But this figure is likely to be much higher in the coming years due to synergies. Especially since Wiz is strong in the enterprise sector, and Gemini Enterprise will likely be one of Alphabet's flagship products in the coming years. So this could offer good synergies.

As if Alphabet's dominant positions in search, streaming and mobile operating systems were not enough, Waymo is also likely to be among the top three companies worldwide in the field of autonomous driving. Although Waymo has lost some excellent employees to Meta, the expertise of the DeepMind (DEEPMIND), Waymo and Gemini developers is still more than sufficient. I also believe that the experience gained with Waymo will be very valuable for the Robotics department. After all, advances in sensors and distance measurements will help to improve the field of robotics.

And I think the robotics sector is not receiving much attention yet, even though there is significant future income potential in the areas of factory robots and home robots. I believe we are still at least five years away from market readiness, but this market will eventually become very important.

Alphabet's TPU

I also believe that Alphabet has a good chance of playing a significant role in the inference area with TPU Ironwood. Alphabet's interchip connect is, in my opinion, superior to Nvidia's (NVDA) NVLink. In general, Ironwood is completely focused on inferencing, which results in better latency, efficiency, and power consumption than the competition.

It is also interesting that Alphabet is offering Ironwood exclusively via its cloud, thereby locking potential customers into its ecosystem, the same as Nvidia does with CUDA.

Overall, I have the feeling that the market does not yet have enough confidence in Alphabet's infrastructure, which the company is currently building. But with the cloud, the undersea cables, Gemini, TPUs and other small applications, Alphabet is currently positioning itself as one of the winners in the field of AI. To me, it looks like an infrastructure empire is quietly and secretly being built here.

Valuation

Alphabet remains the cheapest company in its peer group, alongside Meta. Although not by such a large margin as a few months ago, I still see upward potential to a P/E ratio of 35. In my opinion, this would reflect Alphabet's competitive advantages appropriately.

To exploit time arbitrage, I try to choose a valuation period of three years, as the Wall Street and analysts nowadays often think too short-term. And for December 2028, analysts currently estimate EPS of $14.54.

I think this EPS estimate is too low, as Alphabet's margins will increase more than the market currently expects. My guess is that the EPS will be closer to $15. Probably even higher. But, as we always try to maintain a margin of safety, I'll take $15 as a more conservative estimate.

$15 x 35 P/E = $525 or an upside of ~81% ($525/$290). So Alphabet is still undervalued.

Risks

I think one risk is that Alphabet fundamentally changes its search product through AI and customers do not like this change. Additionally, I think it's still not 100% clear whether LLMs could ultimately lead to lower margins or reduced demand in the search sector. After all, search remains the cash cow of Alphabet, financing the other areas and the other bets. If a portion of the revenue were to disappear here, it would be fatal in the long term. But the longer the search business remains intact, the more Cloud can grow and potentially become a cash cow in the future.

Conclusion

Although I am very satisfied with how Alphabet has performed in recent months, I still believe the shares are undervalued. In my opinion, the market and the analysts still haven't realized how much Alphabet has advanced in the field of AI, or the scale of the infrastructure they are building. Alphabet and its products will tremendously benefit from AI.

data.title: Novo Nordisk: The Sell-Off Is Getting Absolutely Ridiculous
data.description:
Novo Nordisk (NVO) is experiencing one of the most mind baffling disconnects between market cap and actual business fundamentals that I have seen in years. Rather than any actual decline in the company's prospects, investors seem to be solely focused on negative headlines and short-term turbulence.

While some kind of fear is justified trough pricing pressure, competitive noise and negative guidance revisions, the general story is still intact and the bearish sentiment seems to be driven by emotions rather than actual numbers, leading to an exaggerated sell-off.

In my last article on Novo in August I already stated that I believe that Novo Nordisk is currently offering more upside potential than their main competitor Eli Lily and Company (LLY).

For this article, I'll present three different scenarios to cut through the noise and look at the actual numbers and what the current valuation implies for growth and profitability going forward.

But let's first analyze what major events have caused the weakness.

Recent News: The Perfect Strom of Negative Headlines

Price Limitations and Policy Shifts in the United States

Novo Nordisk and U.S. authorities came to an agreement in November to drastically lower Ozempic and Wegovy's list prices beginning in 2026. There were also other list-price reductions reported for popular insulin medications like Fiasp and Tresiba. Predictably, the market sold off on the news.

However, this also offers an opportunity for the company, as expanded coverage is made possible by these price reductions. Most significantly, Medicare Part D will extend coverage for obesity medications. Since private insurers usually match changes in Medicare coverage, they are anticipated to follow.

This is very important as Novo also receives regulatory stability and tariff concessions through this deal, practically eliminating two major risks for Novo. Novo itself anticipates a negligible single-digit impact on worldwide sales in 2026.

Lowered Self-Pay Pricing

Additionally, Novo significantly reduced the self-pay rates for Wegovy and Ozempic, reducing patient expenses down to $199–$349 per month, depending on dosage. Once more, this was seen by the market as margin erosion. However, this is can also be interpreted as a re-capture tactic for cash-paying patients as well as a counterattack against compounding pharmacies.

Lowered Guidance

Due to decreased expectations for their GLP-1 weight loss segment, Novo lowered their revenue forecast for 2025. The stock was severely penalized by investors. However, this should only be a temporary slow-down and certainly doesn't justify the sell-off be have seen. Especially considering that Novo still expects very solid growth in the next months of ~11%.

Increasing Competition

While Novo is currently still the market leader in the obesity segment, their market share has deteriorated constantly over the last few months, as Eli Lilly has improved their products and scaled their production more efficiently.

Now that we understand what caused the sell-off, we can now look at the actual numbers.

Valuation

To evaluate the company we first need some general assumptions. I keep them conservative to add an additional margin of safety.

2025 Revenue: For 2025 I assumed the revenue to stay constant at the current TTM rate of $49.6 billion, therefore assuming no growth for Q4/2025.

Profitability and Cash Conversion: Here I averaged out the last four years and assumed that the respective rate stay constant at that level.

EBIT Margin: 45%

FCF Conversion: 76% of EBIT

WACC: Here I assumed 9.5%, which is comfortably above their current rate of ~7%.

Perpetuity Growth: 3.5% seems fitting considering their historical rates of >11% in the last 10 years as well as the fact that obesity and diabetes treatments are one of the strongest secular growth themes in global healthcare.

Required Growth

Even when considering no growth for any of Novo's segments, the company is currently trading at fair value.

Realistic Growth

Novo is currently operating in three main business segments:

  1. Diabetes Care
  2. Obesity Care
  3. Rare Diseases

For the Diabetes market, experts are currently predicting growth rates in the range of 7% to 9%. The Obesity market on the other hand is expected to grow by 25% to 30% each year.

For some additional margin of safety in the Obesity as well as the Rare Diseases segment, the following market rates seems very well achievable for Novo:

Q3/2025
Segment Revenue [mill. DKK] Share Anticipated Growth
Diabetes Care 49,149 65.6% 8%
GLP-1 36,735 49.0%
Insulin 11,993 16.0%
Obesity care 21,106 28.2% 20%
Wegovy 20,354 27.1%
Saxenda 752 1.0%
Total Rare Diseases 4,721 6.3% 0%

From there on we can weigh the different growth rates with the current revenue share of each segment to get an average expected growth rate for Novo's entire business.

Zero Obesity Growth

In this scenario we expect zero growth, which gives us a top line growth rate of 5.2% only from Diabetes care.

Q3/2025
Segment Revenue [mill. DKK] Share Anticipated Growth
Diabetes Care 49,149 65.6% 8%
GLP-1 36,735 49.0%
Insulin 11,993 16.0%
Obesity care 21,106 28.2% 0%
Wegovy 20,354 27.1%
Saxenda 752 1.0%
Total Rare Diseases 4,721 6.3% 0%
Sum 74,976 Average 5.2%

With these still conservative assumptions, Novo is already looking undervalued by up to 35% as this analysis suggests a fair value market cap of $284 billion.

Market Growth

By assuming the above mentioned market growth rate of 20% for the Obesity segment, Novo should be able to grow their top-line by 10.9% each year. With these assumptions, the company could currently be undervalued by up to 85%.

Risks to Consider

There are however still some risks that investors need to acknowledge. First, there is still a lot of uncertainty around U.S. policy. Although the new price agreement offers predictability for 2026 and beyond, margins may be impacted by subsequent changes, especially wider adoption of lower mandatory pricing could hurt the company in the long-run. Drug and healthcare affordability is a priority for politicians from all parties and GLP-1s are becoming a prominent target.

Another serious threat is competition. Next-generation incretin medicines, both injectable and oral, might change market share or put pressure on prices if Eli Lilly continues to develop quickly. Novo could have to make more price reductions if Lilly's pipeline assets show better tolerability, quicker onset, or better long-term results. Additionally over time, more fragmentation may be introduced by smaller biotech companies who are exploring oral GLP-1s.

Attention should also be paid to operational risks. For Novo, supply-chain strain continues to be a barrier. Refill rates may continue to fluctuate if production growth trails demand, which might result in patient loss and reduced revenue growth.

Conclusion: Why the Sell-Off Is Getting Ridiculous

Novo controls the two of the fastest growing healthcare markets in the world — Diabetes and Obesity Care. Additionally their weight loss drug might also help with treating Alzheimer's Disease. First results of a clinical test on this matter are expected to be announced in the next months. The company is also hiring persons for said segment. This however seems very speculative at the moment.

Despite all this Novo Nordisk is currently priced like they won't grow at all for the next eight years. To restate the mentioned scenarios, the company seems to be severely undervalued right now:

  • zero growth - fairly valued
  • zero Obesity growth - 35% undervalued
  • Growth at just the underlying market rates - 85% undervalued

Therefore I think that the current prolonged sell-off is absolutely ridiculous, which is why I rate the company a Strong Buy right now and doubled my personal position.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: RFI: A Look At This Non-Leveraged 8.6% Yielding Real Estate Fund
data.description:
It has been over two years since we last wrote on Cohen & Steers Total Return Realty (RFI). The last time though, we ran it as a comparison against another closed end fund. Today, we examine it on a standalone basis and tell you why this should be kept on your radar.

The Fund

RFI is a closed end fund that aims to provide its investors a high total return courtesy of investments in real estate securities. For those wondering whether this $300 million fund prioritizes income or capital gain, the prospectus comes to the rescue.

The Fund will seek both current income and capital appreciation (realized and unrealized) with approximately equal emphasis.

Source: Prospectus

A company that that falls under the definition of a real estate investment for RFI is one that derives:

... at least 50% of its revenues from the ownership, construction, financing, manage ment or sale of commercial, industrial, or residential real estate or that has at least 50% of its assets in such real estate (such as REITs).

Source: Prospectus

In terms of type of securities, RFI covers the whole spectrum, including common shares, convertibles, preferreds, debt, rights and warrants. RFI also reserves the right to invest up to 10% of its assets in illiquid securities i.e. those "for which there is no readily available secondary market". As of September 30, most of the funds were invested in common stocks with the rest being allocated between preferreds and bonds.

In terms of sector allocations within the real estate spectrum, Healthcare, Telecommunications and corporate bonds comprised a little more than a third of the portfolio. The fund had a host of other sectors in the portfolio, including Data Centers, Industrial, Self Storage, Residential, and Retail.
