Counting those in the "Other" category, one would be hard pressed to find a type of real estate sector that RFI did not have its hands in. Individually, Welltower Inc. (WELL) was the top holding, followed by a bunch of familiar names like American Tower Corporation (AMT), Digital Realty Trust Inc (DLR), Crown Castle Inc (CCI), and Prologis (PLD)

We have covered the top 5 holdings of RFI on this platform a few times now. Except for CCI, none of them have received a buy rating from our side.

Distributions

RFI has a managed distribution policy, which means their distributions do not vary in the medium term, irrespective of fund net asset value or income changes. The management might lower the payout if they feel that it is unsustainable over the long run. In the case of our protagonist, the monthly payout has been 8 cents since 2016, with a few special distributions thrown in around year end.

Taking into account the current market price of RFI ($11.21), this fund yields 8.6%.

Expenses

RFI has a management fee of 0.70%, and an overall expense ratio (including management fee) of 0.94%.

Considering that this fund has done quite well without taking on leverage, the fees, in our opinion are acceptable.

Performance

RFI presents a comparison of its performance with FTSE Nareit All Equity REITs Index (Linked Index) and a Blended Benchmark. The latter comprises 80% FTSE Nareit All Equity REITs Index and 20% ICE BofA REIT Preferred Securities Index. We will go ahead and ignore the Magnificent Seven driven S&P 500 Index, also shown below. This comparison is probably a throwback to the 2000 - 2020 timeframe when the REITs were crushing the broader market.

RFI has done better than the two real estate focused indices throughout all time frames. The market price has had its fair amount of ups and downs, trading at a discount or premium to its net asset value of its lifetime.

The current NAV is around $11.20 and the market price is almost the same. Based on the premiums it has typically enjoyed over the last year, this fund is in the inexpensive zone.

The above noted Z-Scores also support buying at this price point. However, just because a fund is trading cheaper than it has over the last 12 months, does not automatically make it a buy. If we look at the "Premium/Discount" chart above, there have been long periods where it has been consistently in the discount zone. One has to consider the market conditions to determine whether this is the right time to buy. Yes, of course, the Z-Scores give you an extra edge in picking an entry point, but this is by no means the end all and be all in picking your closed end funds.

Verdict

There are very few CEFs that fall under the "buy and hold" category for us. Most of them take too much leverage for the market valuations we have today. Even amongst the ones that do that do not go "pedal to the metal", and we wrote about one just this week, they tend to underperform the indices. RFI, admittedly, is a different beast. A long history coupled with zero leverage is married here with some outperformance. Granted, that when you run the numbers from Vanguard Real Estate ETF's (VNQ) start date, it isn't that much.

But this is still very impressive for three reasons. First, RFI has a way higher expense ratio than the bare bones VNQ ETF. The latter just charges 0.13%. The second hurdle is that RFI distributes a lot compared to VNQ. VNQ pays out what the underlying companies distribute. Whereas RFI, as noted above, has a managed distribution policy. This makes it much harder for RFI to outperform as it is forced to sell assets at every point and sell more assets when prices are lower. Finally, RFI also holds some lower volatility preferred shares and corporate bonds. These obviously don't appreciate as much in up cycles and have actually had all the levity of lead balloons during 2022-2023. Overall, this fund is an interesting one to keep on your watch list to pick up real estate exposure. We certainly prefer it over chronic horrid performers like abrdn Global Premier Property (AWP).

We even generally prefer it over its brethren Closed End Funds from the same management company as they use leverage. At present, we still like the idea of using an individual stock covered call approach where we can combine the right price and right covered call to generate high income. RFI gets a "hold" from us.

Please note that this is not financial advice. It may seem like it, sound like it, but surprisingly, it is not. Investors are expected to do their own due diligence and consult a professional who knows their objectives and constraints. Most of our articles are not written for "buy and hold" investors. Our articles are definitely not written for investors who ignore capital preservation while focusing only on yield.

data.title: Abbott Laboratories: Not Passing The Test, After Buying Test Maker Exact Sciences
data.description:
Shares of Abbott Laboratories (ABT) have seen a small negative price reaction in response to a larger deal for its smaller diagnostic peer Exact Sciences (EXAS), taking place at a revenue premium on the back of stronger growth, although Exact still has to reach profitability.

The debt and dilution incurred outweighs the added growth profile contribution and while I am usually appealed by setbacks in the share price, I am not seeing enough of a compelling situation here. This prevents me from buying this dip just yet.

Life. To The Fullest

The paragraph header is the mission of Abbott, which aims to reimagine innovation for both a better and healthier world. The company focuses on technologies which allow for accessible and affordable care, helping to live healthier and better lives.

I previously wrote about Abbott here, but for those who are unfamiliar, here is a background: the company is a true giant, employing over 110,000 workers in over 160 countries, with its history dating back to the late 19th century. The company is a huge business, generating over $40 billion, close to $42 billion to be precise.

This is truly a global business with some 60% of sales generated abroad and this huge revenue base being generated across four main segments. The largest of these is a $19 billion medical device segment, coincidentally also being the fastest grower here. This includes activities like rhythm management, hearth failure, vascular, neuromodulation and diabetes care.

This is complemented by a >$9 billion diagnostic segment, a >$8 billion nutrition business and an over $5 billion pharmaceutical business. These activities include molecular services, point of care, rapid diagnostics, pediatric and adult nutrition, etc.

Because of these widespread activities, the business is quite diversified as Abbott has a decent growth track record, in between organic and M&A achievements. Over the past decade, the company has doubled sales, grown margins, while dilution has been limited to about 15% over this period of time.

This has been well reflected in the stock, which has essentially tripled from $40 to $120 over his period of time, as shares already hit these levels in 2021. Ever since, shares have traded in a $100-$140 range over the past five years.

Shedding Perspective

In January of this year, Abbott reported a near 5% increase in reported sales to $42 billion, with underlying revenue growth reported around 9% offset by adverse currency moves. Adjusted net earnings were reported at $8.2 billion, equal to $4.67 per share with that number up twenty-three cents on the year before, as that metric looked quite fair.

The company issued quite an upbeat tone for 2025, seeing organic sales up between 7.5% and 8.5%, with earnings seen up to $5.05-$5.25 per share. An important part of these earnings are paid out to investors, with the dividends being paid out consecutively for over a century. Dividends have been hiked for 53 years in a row, with payouts at the time standing at $0.59 per share per quarter. Largely trading around the $120 mark, shares traded at demanding valuations, at around 23–24 times forward adjusted earnings.

Throughout the year, the company has largely delivered on its expectations, with the full year organic sales guidance for the year now seen at 7.5-8.0%, as of the third quarter, with adjusted earnings still seen at a midpoint of $5.15 per share. A share count of 1.75 billion shares translates into an equity valuation of $219 billion at $125 per share, this including a modest net debt load of around $5 billion.

A resulting $224 billion enterprise valuation values the business at around 5 times sales, with earnings trading at 24 times anticipated earnings.

A Big Deal

Trying to grow the diversified health business even more, Abbott announced the purchase of Exact Sciences for $105 per share, valuing its equity at $21 billion. Exact’s early cancer diagnostics test, including its Cologuard and Oncodetect business, are seen contributing greatly to Abbott's diagnostics business.

Including net debt of $1.8 billion, the total transaction terms call for a nearly $23 billion deal and with revenues seen over $3 billion this year, sales multiples come in just over 7 times sales, however these revenues are increasing at a high teens organic growth rate.

In fact, the deal presentation revealed revenues are seen at $3.2 billion, derived from over 5 million tests performed each year, and while the sales multiple comes at a premium, the overall impact to Abbott's organic growth rate overall is about 50 basis points.

With Exact Sciences barely breaking even on an operating basis here, the deal is expected to have a twenty cent dilutive impact to 2026 earnings per share, with accretion seen from 2028 and beyond. This is driven by organic growth and a targeted $100 million in cost synergies. With pro forma net debt seen moving up to $28 billion, the company sees pro forma net debt around 2.7 times EBITDA.

And Now?

Shares of Abbott fell from $129 to $125 per share upon the news announcement, with shares down $7 billion on the back of the premium offered in this $23 billion deal. This decline coincides largely with the premium offered for the shares of Exact, after shares traded in the $60s ahead of takeover rumors.

In between all this, I think that the reaction to the deal looks quite fair. Right now Abbott has been taking on quite some leverage and while the payout ratio is relatively high, solid organic growth is impressive enough as I do not have leverage concerns.

In between all this, the reaction looks largely fair, with the premium reflected in a fall in Abbott's shares, in part as leverage will jump, but also as earnings multiples will increase from 24 times to 25 times was well, despite the lower share price, as the earnings estimates have been cut quite a bit following dilution incurred.

In between all this, the reaction looks largely fair. While Abbott has proven to be a solid long term value creator, expectations remain a bit elevated even as shares have been making inroads, after trading flat for years.

To see appeal, I need to see a slightly smaller premium attached to the shares, i.e. a long period of share price stagnation, or moves closer to the $100 mark, before getting appealed to the long term (dividend) value creator.

data.title: RFMZ: Best Value Of The Lineup
data.description:
Written by Nick Ackerman, co-produced by Stanford Chemist

RiverNorth offers a lineup of municipal bond-focused closed-end funds; these take an approach of investing in both individual bonds and other muni-focused CEFs. The strategy is an attempt to trade around based on the overall environment for CEF investing. These generally aren't focused on providing high levels of tax-free distributions, though that is a part of the distribution characteristic naturally from investing in munis. The classification that gets the largest allocation is often nondividend distributions (aka return of capital).

That can come with its own potential tax benefits, but it is not the same as federally and state tax-exempt distributions that some investors may be looking for. That's why I've continued to believe that these funds are better for traders and not a focus for investors wanting tax-free distributions from muni exposure.

The one fund that is currently trading at the widest discount for this basket of funds is the RiverNorth Flexible Municipal Income II (RFMZ), making it look like the more attractive option at the moment. However, RiverNorth Flexible Municipal Income (RFM) and RiverNorth Opportunity Municipal Income Fund (RMI) also aren't too far behind in terms of absolute discount.

RFMZ Basics

  • 1-Year Z-score: -1.02
  • Discount/Premium: -10.98%
  • Distribution Yield: 8.03%
  • Expense Ratio: 2.38%
  • Leverage: 39.9%
  • Managed Assets: $593.8 million
  • Structure: Term (anticipated liquidation date Feb. 26, 2036)

RFMZ's investment objective is "to provide current income exempt from regular U.S. federal income taxes, with a secondary objective of total return."

In an attempt to achieve this, the fund will "allocate the Fund's managed assets between two principal strategies: Tactical Municipal Closed-End Fund Strategy (managed by RiverNorth) and Municipal Bond Income Strategy (managed by MacKay Shields)."

In determining the allocation, it is by the CEF environment. Below is a rough idea of how the allocations will work at any given time.

With RFMZ, the fund is highly leveraged, and that isn't all that unusual for a muni CEF—after all, muni bonds are extremely safe.

That said, the underlying CEFs the fund will invest in can also be leveraged highly. So that is leverage on leverage, as well as fees on fees, and also potential discount on discount volatility. That can make these funds quite risky and volatile compared to traditional muni exposure or even holding a muni-focused CEF on its own.

For RFMZ, when including borrowing costs for its own leverage, the fund's total expense ratio comes up to 4.75%. That has eased from the 5.08% in the prior fiscal year on the back of Fed rate cuts.

Performance - Widest Discount of Peers

Since our last update, the fund has delivered positive total returns. It had underperformed the S&P 500 Index, but that wouldn't be an appropriate benchmark given the muni focus of RFMZ.

When looking at the total returns of RFMZ over the longer term compared to its sister funds, on a total share price basis, it comes in second place. That is just behind the RiverNorth Managed Duration Municipal Income Fund II (RMMZ).

(Note: RMM is not included in the chart because Ycharts is missing the data.)

We are limited in the timeline of looking at historical performance by RMMZ, which is the last of these funds that were launched. The fund's inception date is February 10, 2022. While the total NAV returns of the funds are quite close, RMMZ has come out ahead on that front as well.

That is also the one that carries the narrowest discount—which could suggest investors believe that outperformance could continue. With that, investors could question whether they think that outperformance is going to continue and if that makes up for the differential in the valuation between the two funds. These funds are highly correlated, and RMMZ has not pulled away enough historically for me to consider it a slam dunk win.

(Note: RMM's discount sits at 8.11%.)

On another note, RFMZ and RMMZ are both term-structured funds where they are anticipated to be liquidated in the future. Alternatively, and what is more likely, they will go through a tender offer first in an attempt to go perpetual. After the tender offer, if there are at least $100 million in net assets, the fund can remove its term structure and convert to a perpetual fund. In the case of RFMZ, the term date is Feb. 26, 2036. That's well off from now and isn't too material to consider for the time being.

Beginning one year before February 26, 2036 (the "Termination Date"), the Fund may begin to liquidate all or a portion of the Fund's portfolio. At the discretion of the Board of Directors ("Board"), the Termination Date may be extended (i) once for up to one year, and (ii) once for up to an additional six months; or Within twelve months prior to the Termination Date, the Fund may conduct a tender offer to purchase 100% of the then outstanding shares. Following the completion of the tender offer, the Fund must have at least $100 million of net assets. The Board may then eliminate the Termination Date and convert the Fund to a perpetual structure upon the affirmative vote of a majority of the Board.

Managed Distribution Provides Elevated Payout

RFMZ currently maintains a 6.75% NAV distribution rate that is reset annually. However, they have made adjustments to this level in the past, so this payout isn't necessarily set in stone.

At this time, the NAV rate comes to 7.15%, so that would indicate a cut will be coming for next year unless they change the rate for the policy or we see some very strong performance in the last month or so of the year.

In general, the fund is banking on capital gains to fund its distribution, which would come from trading its underlying muni CEFs. In the last fiscal year that ended June 30, 2025, the fund had net investment income of $0.40. That can be compared to the fund paying out $1.03 in total distributions. During the fiscal year, the fund also had unrealized/realized losses of $0.99 per share.

On a more positive note, in a lower rate environment, that can help to ease borrowing costs on the short side, and if long-term rates come down, that can also help push underlying bond prices higher. RFMZ is leveraged, and its underlying CEF holdings are also often leveraged, making lower borrowing costs even more impactful for the fund. With that, we have been and are continuing to be in a more favorable rate environment for the fund to operate.

However, as noted about the NAV rate being elevated compared to its managed policy level set at the start of the year, it wasn't enough to cover the payout. Beyond overpaying for the distribution, the fund's deep discount also helps to elevate the market distribution rate even higher—pushing it to 8.03% currently.

Given the lack of distribution coverage and capital gains to cover the payout, it isn't too surprising that we see a heavier classification of the distribution as return of capital.

Generally, ROC is often a very misunderstood thing when it comes to CEFs, as some investors assume it is automatically a negative. There are cases where ROC distributions can show up, but the fund's NAV is still rising or staying flat.

In this case, as the NAV has been trending lower, it would indicate destructive ROC. Given the elevated rate that the fund is attempting to pay as well, it seems that there is likely always to be some destructive ROC. RMM was a fund that existed in 2020 and 2021, when the Fed funds rate was pegged at or near zero. Even during that time, the fund was still paying a distribution level over what it could generate in NII.

However, to be fair, most of the destruction came shortly after the initial launch when the Fed was raising its benchmark rate aggressively. Given that muni bonds are extremely interest rate sensitive, with RFMZ's estimated unhedged duration at 11.3 years, and long rates were also rising, the hit makes sense. With the estimated hedged duration, it comes down to 7.6 years, but that is still meaningful. With that, the erosion over the last few years has been relatively minimal compared to that.

RFMZ's Portfolio

At this time, the fund's strategy is weighted more toward the individual bond income strategy over the CEF sleeve. Within the CEF portfolio, the weighted average discount comes to -6.6%. That would be where the discount on discount comes from, as RFMZ is already trading at a 10%+ discount level too. As expected, we can also see that most of the portfolio is listed at investment grade.

The largest state exposure that the fund carries is to New York, California, and Illinois. This is generally the case, given that these are the states with some of the highest GDP and, thus, the states that issue the most.

When looking at the top positions, Seeking Alpha notes that it is quite concentrated, comprising around 43%. Of course, that isn't necessarily that bad when you also have that fund of funds sleeve within the portfolio. In fact, the largest holding is the Nuveen Quality Muni Income Fund (NAD), which itself has over 1000 underlying positions.

Conclusion

RFMZ is an interesting fund given its deep discount, which can make it the best of its current sister funds that all invest rather similarly. Historically, the funds tend to have high correlation, though RMMZ did get a bit of a boost in total returns relative to the rest of the lineup. However, the biggest "boost" was in the form of the fund's total share price returns, thanks to the fund seeing its discount narrow to the shallowest level. On a total NAV return basis, the gap is much less material.

That said, I don't view these funds as great ways to gain muni exposure for investors seeking tax-exempt distributions, as they are more trading vehicles. They've also historically provided a minority of the distribution being classified as tax-exempt; rather, return of capital has been the largest classification.

Editor's Note: This article covers one or more microcap stocks. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: 4 Reasons Stocks Plunged And "The Big Buy Indicator" That Was Just Triggered
data.description:
The S&P 500 Index (SPY) and other major indexes have dropped for the past few days, and the losses are starting to add up, with the major indexes now down between 5% and 8% from the recent highs. I have been bearish on stocks for a while now, and over two weeks ago I wrote this article suggesting that all the ingredients we needed for a stock market correction were present and that I believed it would happen soon. Now that we have had a 5% to 8% decline in some major indexes, I believe the market is in a healthier position, and this reset could actually help fuel a year-end "Santa Claus Rally" which I just wrote about in this article. This market plunge seems to have taken some investors off guard, so I want to review the reasons why it happened and also why it might be smart to do some selective buying in anticipation of a year-end rally. With this in mind, let's take a closer look at the sell-off:

What Sparked The Sell Off

I see 4 major reasons for the recent market plunge, and this includes: the Fed, froth, Japan, and Bitcoin. In recent days, bond yields in Japan have jumped, and this is causing stress for some global investors who have used the "Yen Carry Trade" over the years, whereby they borrow at low rates in Japan and deploy the cash elsewhere. The rise in Japanese interest rates has spooked some investors and caused some selling pressure on certain global assets. This situation with Japanese rates was already causing some market stress, but then on November 19, the Federal Reserve released minutes that showed a rate cut in December is not a sure thing. This appeared to trigger more concerns in the equity market and in particular with Bitcoin, which saw a renewed plunge shortly after investors realized the Fed might not cut rates at the next meeting, which is on December 9 and 10.

In terms of speculative froth and Bitcoin, which go hand in hand in my opinion, there was just too much of it. Seeing high-flying tech stocks and meme stocks surge in recent weeks was one sign of speculative excess. But it was also obvious that some investors were going far out on a limb when you see how much margin debt has been rising. Margin debt recently rose to an all-time record of around $1.2 trillion. In my opinion, and as I warned in some of my recent articles, this level of borrowing was the perfect setup for a market correction because that has historically been the case when margin debt gets this high. High levels of margin debt and speculation in assets like Bitcoin show investors have become complacent, and complacency is typically present just before a market decline.

Nvidia Is Not The Problem, But Bitcoin Still Might Be

Nvidia (NVDA) released a stellar Q3 earnings report and solid guidance on November 19th. This initially sparked a rebound in tech stocks and the market in general. However, on the morning of November 20th, even though the stock market was enjoying a strong rally, Bitcoin started to turn lower. There have been reports of margin calls with many accounts that hold Bitcoin, and that seems to be putting forced selling pressure on the cryptocurrency sector, which has been dropping for weeks now. Bitcoin hit a record high in October of about $125,000, and it has been downhill almost ever since. I believe Bitcoin holders also frequently own popular tech stocks like Nvidia, and many of them might have been forced to sell their tech stocks in order to shore up their portfolios and reduce risks.

Bitcoin has become a leveraged "asset" and it could continue to decline and put more selling pressure on the stock market. This recent article suggests significant margin call selling has hit the cryptocurrency markets, and it mentions that Coinbase (COIN) and other exchanges have enabled leveraged bets on Bitcoin, which are now being unwound, it states:

"This expansion of leverage has accelerated risk transmission. Traders can now gain exposure of $100 in Bitcoin with only $1 of collateral. When markets move downward, losses are magnified, positions breach margin thresholds, and holdings are forcibly liquidated. As Nansen research analyst Nicolai Søndergaard notes, investors may have more ways to amplify returns, but poor risk management now carries sharper consequences."

I believe Bitcoin continues to pose a risk to the financial markets as it is hard to know how much margin call selling might be left and also how much worse the decline might get. As shown in the chart below, Bitcoin is now approaching the lows it hit in April when the Liberation Day tariff announcement roiled the markets. This suggests there could be more downside ahead for Bitcoin, but it is very oversold now, so we might see some investors with laser eyes step in to buy this dip.

Where Things Stand Technically

The S&P 500 Index (SPY) is now about 5% below the recent all-time highs, although other indexes are off even more. Many investors have been rewarded by buying the dip in recent years, and for some, a 5% pullback is where they start buying. Because of this, we might see the market stabilize around current levels, especially if Bitcoin stabilizes as well. The S&P 500 Index is now trading below the 50-day moving average, which is represented by the blue trend line on the chart. The 200-day moving average is represented by the red trend line, and reaching this level would represent an additional decline of about 7%, which I do not see as being likely at this time.

Here's The "Big Buy Indicator" That I See

The CNN Fear & Greed Index is an indicator that I follow because I have seen it work as a contrarian indicator. For example, when the index is in the greed zone, I view this as a time to consider selling stocks, and when it is at extreme fear levels, I force myself to do some buying because asset prices are typically "on sale" when investors are fearful. As shown below, this index is at just 6 right now. This is indicating extreme fear levels, and this is historically a buy signal for contrarian investors. When other investors are this fearful, I want to be buying stocks.

Reasons For Optimism And To Start Buying

This decline in the stock market has been rapid, and this could just be a blip and a healthy reset that cleanses the market of some of the excesses. Stocks are now oversold and could be due for a bounce for technical reasons as well.

This decline could now set the market up for a year-end Santa Claus rally, which history shows is often the case.

I believe the Fed will cut in January if it does not cut in December. After this sell-off, the Fed might feel more pressure to cut rates and feel better about it since some of the froth and excess has just been wrung out of the market and out of Bitcoin. The odds suggest that there is a 66% chance of a Fed rate cut in January, and if investors start to focus on that, it could set the stage for a year-end rally.

Some stocks have been beaten down due to tax-loss selling and, in some cases just took another hit with this broad market sell off. This has created some bargain buying opportunities and tax-loss selling is going to fade over the next few weeks as the end of the year rapidly approaches. These beaten-down stocks could be poised to rebound sharply and help lift the stock market.

Thanksgiving week is typically a strong one for stocks. History shows that the S&P 500 Index typically rises 75% of the time, with a gain of nearly 1% on average.

What I Am Focused On Buying Now

At this time of year I usually focus on buying stocks that appear to be getting hit with tax-loss selling. This includes stocks like Whirlpool (WHR) which hedge fund legend David Tepper recently announced a very major stake in. I am also buying stocks like Home Depot (HD), Centene (CNC) and Calavo Growers (CVGW). In terms of tech stocks, Amazon (AMZN) is now on the top of my buy list since it just reported a strong Q3, and yet the stock gave up its recent gains which makes it very attractive to me. I see the sell-off in stocks like Amazon as being unwarranted and I believe Amazon shares can rebound into Thanksgiving week as investors focus on the massive amounts of money being spent by holiday shoppers on "Black Friday" and the upcoming holiday season.

Potential Risks To Consider

The amount of leverage in Bitcoin and cryptocurrencies has grown large and since this is a relatively new "asset" class and some of the financial products within it have not been tested extensively, so the sell-off could get worse. Margin call selling might not be done yet when it comes to Bitcoin or stocks. Japan remains a risk worth considering, as interest rates in that country could continue to move higher. However, the economy in Japan is showing strong growth, so interest rates are rising for a good reason.

In Summary

I have been bearish recently as I expected a market sell-off; however, now that we have seen a lot of speculative assets decline significantly, I think the market is looking healthier since some froth has been removed. I believe this sell-off makes it easier for the Fed to lower interest rates in either December or January. I also believe that since this time of year is typically strong for stocks, this reset in valuations makes a year-end rally more likely. As a contrarian investor, I see the extreme fear in the market as a time to start doing some buying, especially stocks like Amazon.
